figures.caption: FIG. 8. The imaginary part of the direct ATI ionization time computed at (p∥, 0) with the PINN solver detailed in Sec. IV for the elliptically polarized fields shown in Fig. 1, [panels (a)-(d)]. For each field, multiple windows corresponding to each distinct solution are taken and denoted by crosses and circles. Three different intensities I = 0.5 × 1014W/cm2, 1 × 1014W/cm2 and 2× 1014W/cm2 have been used, denoted by blue, orange and black lines respectively. The corresponding PMDs for each field are displayed in panels (a’)-(d’) with I = 2× 1014W/cm2 and taking all windows and multiple cycles of the fields. Panels (e) and (e’) show the mean square error (MSE) in the imaginary time at (p∥, 0) and for the entire 2D momentum grid with the ϵ = 0.3 case, respectively. All other field parameters are the same as in the associated panels of Fig. 1.

TABLE IV. Relevant ionization windows (denoted by the ×,⃝,△ and + symbols) associated with each of the events for the bichromatic driving fields displayed in Fig. 1(c)-(c”’), from the left column to right column, respectively. The first row gives the field parameters, the second row gives the time interval for which these events occur, the third row states the signs of the parallel momenta associated with each event and the final row shows a schematic of the events found in each window.

FIG. 7. The imaginary part of the direct ATI ionization time computed at (p∥, 0) with the PINN solver detailed in Sec. IV for the bichromatic fields shown in Fig. 1, [panels (a)-(d)]. For each field, multiple windows corresponding to each distinct solution are taken and denoted by crosses, circles, triangles and plus signs. Three different intensities I = 0.5 × 1014W/cm2, 1× 1014W/cm2 and 2× 1014W/cm2 have been used, denoted by blue, orange and black lines respectively. The corresponding PMDs for each field are displayed in panels (a’)-(d’) with I = 2×1014W/cm2 and taking all windows and multiple cycles of the fields. Panels (e) and (e’) show the mean square error (MSE) in the imaginary time at (p∥, 0) and for the entire 2D momentum grid with the (ω, 2ω, ϕ = 2π) case, respectively. All other field parameters are the same as in the associated panels of Fig. 1.

FIG. 2. Schematic of the physics-informed neural network with 3 hidden layers. Inputs comprise of the momentum components (p∥, p⊥) and field parameters γ. The network outputs the real and imaginary parts of the complex saddle time, (tR, tI). Training minimizes a physics residual Lphysics derived from the saddle-point equation until the mean square error falls below a threshold accuracy; a supervised data term Ldata can be included when labeled solutions are available, but is omitted here.

FIG. 5. The imaginary [panels (a),(a’)] and real [panels (b), (b’)] parts of the direct ATI ionization time computed at (p∥, 0) with the PINN solver detailed in Sec. IV, for two windows [first and second rows respectively, and distinguished using crosses and circles] in one cycle of the monochromatic field given by Eq. (9). Three different intensities I = 0.5 × 1014W/cm2, 1× 1014W/cm2 and 2× 1014W/cm2 have been used, denoted by blue, orange and black lines respectively. Panels (c), (c’) show the PMD for the smallest and largest intensities respectively, while panels (d) and (d’) display the mean square error in the real and imaginary parts of the time for all intensities, respectively. All other field parameters are the same as in the associated panel of Fig. 1.

FIG. 6. The imaginary part of the direct ATI ionization time computed at (p∥, 0) with the PINN solver detailed in Sec. IV for the few-cycle pulses shown in Fig. 1 [panels (a)-(c)]. For each field, multiple windows corresponding to each distinct solution are taken and denoted by crosses, circles and, triangles. Three different intensities I = 0.5× 1014W/cm2, 1× 1014W/cm2 and 2× 1014W/cm2 have been used, denoted by blue, orange and black lines respectively. The corresponding PMDs for each field are displayed in panels (a’)-(c’) with I = 2 × 1014W/cm2 and taking all windows and multiple cycles of the fields. Panels (d) and (d’) show the mean square error (MSE) in the imaginary time at (p∥, 0) and for the entire 2D momentum grid with ϕ1 = 64◦, respectively. All other field parameters are the same as in the associated panels of Fig. 1.

TABLE II. Ablation study for model selection in the monochromatic field. ‘Loss’ denotes the final physics-residual objective under a fixed epoch budget. ‘Runtime’ is the corresponding wall-clock training time measured on the same hardware. The windowed parameterization yields the largest improvement, indicating that explicit root control is critical in multi-root regimes.

FIG. 3. Window configuration for the x-direction saddle-time solutions in a four-fold bicircular field. The scatter points represent the complex-time solutions of the saddle-point equation. The four windows, labeled W1–W4, define the prescribed output regions used in the windowed parameterization. For each window, the black dashed vertical line marks the window center, while the colored dashed lines and arrows indicate the corresponding window range. The upper and lower panels show the two sets of solution branches in the xdirection.

FIG. 1. Electric field (blue solid line) and the corresponding vector potential (orange dashed line as functions of time for the (a) monochromatic field, (b-b”) few-cycle pulse with N = 4.3 and carrier-envelope phases ϕ = 65◦, 155◦ and 245◦, (c-c”’) (ω, 2ω) and (ω, 3ω) bichromatic linearly polarized fields with ratio of field amplitudes ξ = 0.8 and relative phase ϕ as indicated in the panels, (d-d”’) elliptically polarized fields with ellipticity ε = 0.1, 0.3, 0.5 and 0.7 and (e-e”) counter-rotating bicircular fields with identical amplitude, relative phase ϕ and commensurate frequency s as indicated in the panels. For all fields, we use wavelength λ = 800nm (ω = 0.057) a.u., and (peak) intensity I = 1.0× 1014 W/cm2.

FIG. 9. The imaginary part of the direct ATI ionization time computed at (p∥, 0) with the PINN solver detailed in Sec. IV for the bicircular fields shown in Fig. 1, [panels (a)-(c)]. For each field, multiple windows corresponding to each distinct solution are taken and denoted by crosses, circles, triangles and plus signs. Three different intensities I = 0.5×1014W/cm2, 1×1014W/cm2 and 2 × 1014W/cm2 have been used, denoted by blue, orange and black lines respectively. The corresponding PMDs for each field are displayed in panels (a’)-(c’) with I = 2× 1014W/cm2 and taking all windows and multiple cycles of the fields. Panels (d) and (d’) show the mean square error (MSE) in the imaginary time at (p∥, 0) and for the entire 2D momentum grid with the (ω, 2ω, ϕ = 2π) case, respectively. All other field parameters are the same as in the associated panels of Fig. 1
